claimed_contribution: The paper identifies parenting motivation as a novel antecedent of within-category variety seeking, showing that it reduces variety seeking.
It proposes and tests feelings of time crunch as the psychological mechanism linking parenting motivation to reduced variety seeking.
It maps boundary conditions for the effect, showing attenuation when time-saving parenting support is salient, when shopping time is perceived as ample, and when brand loyalty is low.
context: The paper studies consumer shopping and brand choice for everyday products, combining real purchase behavior and controlled choice tasks. The setting includes U.S. household scanner-panel purchases and online/lab decisions in product categories such as beverages, pens, hand soap, shampoo, and fabric softener.
design_and_data: The authors run six studies: one large panel-data analysis and five experiments. Study 1 uses NielsenIQ household panel data (2004-2015) with a within-household difference-in-differences design around transition to parenthood (2,049 households; 1,511,092 household-trip-category observations) and measures variety seeking as the ratio of unique UPCs to items purchased within category per trip. Study 2 experimentally activates parenting motivation (infant-face imagination) versus active and inactive controls and measures both variety seeking and perceived shopping time crunch. Studies 3-5 (nonparent student samples in 3, 4, 5a; parent online sample in 5b) test moderation-of-process via parenting support, perceived shopping time availability, and brand loyalty proxies (self-brand connection, brand attachment), using hypothetical and real/incentive-compatible choices.
main_findings: Consumers show lower within-category variety seeking after becoming parents, and experimentally induced parenting motivation also decreases variety seeking.
Feelings of time crunch increase under parenting motivation and mediate the reduction in variety seeking.
The effect weakens when time-saving parenting support is salient, when consumers are led to perceive ample shopping time, and when consumers have low loyalty to available brands.
one_line_summary: Across panel and experimental studies, activating parenting motivation makes consumers feel more time-crunched and therefore choose less variety within product categories.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior work on parenting motivation by moving beyond previously studied outcomes to show effects on routine consumption variety-seeking decisions. It also refines variety-seeking research by adding a social-role based antecedent and process account centered on time crunch, with clear boundary conditions.
research_gap: Prior variety-seeking research has focused mainly on traits, cognitive activation, and shopping environments, with limited attention to whether consumers' social roles shape variety seeking. The parenting-motivation literature had rarely examined consumer decision making and had not established feelings of time crunch as a mechanism for variety-seeking outcomes.
research_question: How does parenting motivation influence consumers' tendency to seek variety in product choices? Through what mechanism does this effect occur, and under what conditions is it strengthened or weakened?
